clips.viral_hook: this movement is the beginnings, I think, of bringing active equity management back into style
clips.transcript: this movement is the beginnings, I think, of bringing active equity management back into style because we're talking about events that should move stocks. And if we have more people looking for and at those events, they're going to be interested in investing in some of these opportunities and maybe taking a little bit out of that passive portfolio that is tied to the broader based indexes. So I'm pretty excited about this. This could be the beginning of a resurgence in active equity management.
clips.description: Cathie Wood explains how prediction markets are set to disrupt passive index funds by re-introducing active equity management.

clips.viral_hook: we believe that this market could explode up to five trillion dollars in notional volume
clips.transcript: so over the next few years, we believe that this market could explode up to five trillion dollars in notional volume. Now, right now, Cal, she is on an annualized run rate to do around one hundred and twenty one hundred and thirty billion dollars in notional volume for this year. So we're talking about a massive explosion of where this total market is headed. But when you put it into the perspective of the total derivatives market, which in 2024 was around seven hundred and fifteen trillion dollars in notional volume, you're only talking about 70 basis points, right? That five five trillion dollar figure over 715. So it's a drop in the bucket compared to where traditional derivatives sit today. And as you point out, I think this is an expansion of the addressable market that derivatives don't necessarily give you today in terms of the risk profile.
clips.description: Nick Gruss breaks down the massive growth potential of prediction markets compared to the traditional derivatives industry.

clips.viral_hook: will the U.S. federal deficit to GDP ratio drop below 5 percent for fiscal year 2026?
clips.transcript: will the U.S. federal deficit to GDP ratio drop below 5 percent for fiscal year 2026? Now, fiscal year means through the end of September. So the year through September of 26. And as you can see here, as the war in the Middle East has extended, the odds that the GDP, the debt deficit to GDP ratio drops below 5 percent. Those have dropped, understandably. So let's take a look at where we are. So here is the GDP. It's at about 5.12 percent. And you can see it was moving in the right direction before the war. And we really thought it was going to drop below 5 percent and head for that 3 percent, which is Treasury Secretary Besant's goal for the end of 28. In fact, we thought that it was going to drop below that 3 percent well before before the end of 28.
clips.description: Cathie Wood analyzes the U.S. federal deficit-to-GDP ratio and the impact of geopolitical conflict on fiscal goals.

clips.viral_hook: our sort of deflationary undercurrent thesis around technology
clips.transcript: our sort of deflationary undercurrent thesis around technology. And we saw this in the 50 years that ended in the late 20s, the late 20s in the 1900s, which was also ripe with innovation. So telephone, electricity, internal combustion engine, all riding on top of the railroads that were built out in the 1800s. Well, the equivalent to the railroads this time is the internet in the digital world. And now we have robotics, energy storage, AI, very importantly AI, blockchain technology, and multiomics technology. All of those are highly deflationary trends. And could the yield curve be picking up on that? We think it might.
clips.description: Cathie Wood explains why AI, robotics, and energy storage are driving powerful deflationary trends in the modern economy.

clips.viral_hook: the people who are willing to trade or make a prediction, they actually think the dollar is going to go up
clips.transcript: they actually think the dollar is going to go up. And that goes against conventional wisdom. Now we agree that it is going to go up, mostly because of what I just mentioned about foreign direct investment and our capital-friendly, business-friendly tax policies. Also, deregulation is, we think, going to increase the return on investment in the United States relative to that in the rest of the world. That is dollar positive. And we take this chart all the way back into the early 80s. Why? It is because we like to illustrate what happened during Reaganomics. And we think Trumponomics resembles greatly, actually, Reaganomics. And look what happened to the dollar back then.
clips.description: Contrary to popular belief, Cathie Wood explains why the US Dollar is likely to strengthen based on tax and trade policy.

clips.viral_hook: really, you should be comparing debt to equity
clips.transcript: really, you should be comparing debt to equity. So equity is the black line, debt is the purple, and GDP is the red. And if we go to the next chart, you will see that while debt to GDP is above one here, the one ratio, you can see debt to equity is at 0.5. And actually it's very low by historical standards.
clips.description: Cathie Wood argues why standard debt-to-GDP metrics are flawed and why debt-to-equity provides a better picture of US finances.

clips.viral_hook: Entry-level jobs are not there for college graduates the way they have been in the past
clips.transcript: Entry-level jobs are not there for college graduates the way they have been in the past. So unit labor costs, this is underlying inflation, 2.4%. What's interesting about that is, if you look at the TIPS market, that's what the TIPS market says inflation is going to be, in other words, no real improvement from here. If we're right and productivity starts scaling three and into next year four or five percent, this unit labor cost growth rate will come down and underlying inflation will come down.
clips.description: Cathie Wood discusses how AI is transforming the job market, specifically for recent college graduates.

clips.topic: Why Consumers are in a Funk
clips.viral_hook: Sentiment is terrible, as measured by the University of Michigan
clips.transcript: Sentiment is terrible, as measured by the University of Michigan. And these are the three income levels, low, mid, and high. The lower income level in the last month dropped back to its recent low. So, affordability, unfortunately, still a massive problem. could really impact the midterm elections. Unemployment. Here's confidence in as measured by jobs easy to get minus jobs hard to get. Confidence is deteriorating here. Now a bright spot is that the entry-level jobs, remember that unemployment rate went before revisions up to 11 percent. It's now reversing to eight and a half percent.
clips.description: Cathie Wood analyzes why consumer sentiment remains at historic lows despite some labor market improvements.
